meta.certificationName: Microsoft Certified: Azure AI Apps and Agents Developer Associate (AI-103)
meta.title: MS-AI103-1: Foundry Agents, RAG and Responsible AI

questions.stem: A team wants to build a customer support chatbot that answers questions about their product documentation. The documentation is updated weekly. Which approach is MOST appropriate for keeping the chatbot's knowledge current without retraining a model?

options.text: Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with a vector index refreshed from the updated documentation
Fine-tune an open-source LLM on the documentation corpus each week
Include the full documentation in every prompt to the LLM
Use a proprietary LLM with a large context window so no retrieval is needed

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because RAG decouples the knowledge base from the model weights, allowing the vector index to be refreshed with new documents without retraining. The retrieval component always fetches the latest indexed content at query time.

**B** is incorrect because fine-tuning weekly is expensive and slow, and fine-tuned knowledge can still become stale between retraining cycles.

**C** is incorrect because documentation corpora are typically too large to fit in any context window, and cost per query would be prohibitive.

**D** is incorrect because even large context windows cannot hold enterprise documentation corpora, and the model's parametric knowledge would still have a training cutoff date.

[Retrieval Augmented Generation with Azure AI Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)

questions.stem: An engineer is designing a RAG pipeline. After evaluating the system, they find that retrieved chunks are often irrelevant to the user's query. Which of the following changes to the chunking strategy is MOST likely to improve retrieval precision?

options.text: Increase chunk size from 128 tokens to 1024 tokens to capture more context
Reduce chunk overlap from 20 tokens to 0 tokens to avoid duplicate content
Switch from fixed-size chunking to semantic chunking that splits on natural topic boundaries
Increase the number of retrieved chunks (top-k) from 3 to 20

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because semantic chunking creates chunks that correspond to coherent topics, making each chunk more likely to match a specific type of query and improving retrieval precision.

**A** is incorrect because very large chunks reduce precision — a 1024-token chunk may contain many topics and will be retrieved for queries that only match a small portion of it.

**B** is incorrect because removing overlap can cause important context that spans chunk boundaries to be missed, hurting recall without reliably improving precision.

**D** is incorrect because increasing top-k adds noise by pulling in more potentially irrelevant chunks, hurting precision even if recall improves.

[Chunking strategies in Azure AI Search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-integrated-vectorization-ai-studio)

questions.stem: An Azure AI developer is configuring an Azure AI Search vector index. Documents are updated continuously in a source data store, and the engineer wants index changes to propagate automatically. Which Azure AI Search indexer configuration supports this?

options.text: A push-model index where the application calls the Documents Index API on every update
A pull-model indexer scheduled to run on a recurring interval or triggered by a change-detection policy
A Flat L2 index with exhaustive nearest-neighbor search
An HNSW approximate nearest-neighbor index with no indexer

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Azure AI Search pull-model indexers can be scheduled or triggered by a change-detection policy on the data source, automatically propagating updates to the index without application code managing each write.

**A** is incorrect because a push-model index requires the application to explicitly call the REST API to add, update, or delete documents — there is no automatic sync from the source.

**C** is incorrect because L2 (Euclidean distance) describes a similarity metric for vector search, not an index synchronization mechanism.

**D** is incorrect because HNSW is a graph-based approximate nearest-neighbor algorithm, not a data synchronization strategy.

[Azure AI Search indexers overview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/search-indexer-overview)

questions.stem: A team is evaluating embedding similarity metrics for a legal document search system. Documents range from 1,000 to 5,000 words. Which similarity metric is generally preferred for normalized embedding vectors and why?

options.text: L2 (Euclidean) distance, because it captures absolute positional differences between vectors
Dot product, because it is computationally cheaper than cosine similarity
Cosine similarity, because it measures the angle between vectors and is invariant to vector magnitude
Jaccard similarity, because it accounts for token overlap between document embeddings

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because cosine similarity measures the angular distance between vectors, making it invariant to the magnitude of the embedding. For text similarity, documents of different lengths can still be semantically close even if their raw vector magnitudes differ.

**B** is partially true that dot product is cheaper, and for unit-normalized vectors dot product equals cosine similarity — but when vectors are not normalized, dot product conflates magnitude with direction, making it less reliable.

**A** is incorrect because L2 distance is magnitude-sensitive and less appropriate for semantic text search, particularly when documents vary in length.

**D** is incorrect because Jaccard similarity applies to set-based representations (e.g., bag of words), not dense embedding vectors.

[Vector similarity in Azure AI Search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-ranking)

questions.stem: A startup wants to deploy a generative AI application with the lowest possible inference cost and does not require top-tier response quality. Which LLM selection strategy is MOST aligned with these goals on Azure AI Foundry?

options.text: GPT-4 via Azure OpenAI Service for maximum quality
A small open-source model (e.g., Phi-3 Mini or Mistral 7B) deployed via Azure AI Foundry Model-as-a-Service with pay-per-token pricing
GPT-4o via Azure OpenAI Service for its large context window
Fine-tune GPT-4 on proprietary data to reduce the number of API calls

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because smaller open-source models like Phi-3 Mini or Mistral 7B have dramatically lower inference costs and can be deployed on smaller compute. Pay-per-token models from Azure OpenAI Service are more expensive for high-volume use cases where quality is not the primary concern.

**A** is incorrect because GPT-4 is one of the most expensive models available and does not meet the lowest-cost requirement.

**C** is incorrect because GPT-4o still carries a premium cost compared to small open-source models.

**D** is incorrect because GPT-4 fine-tuning incurs both fine-tuning compute costs and ongoing premium inference costs, which would increase total cost.

[Model catalog in Azure AI Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/how-to/model-catalog-overview)

questions.stem: A team is designing a RAG system for a technical knowledge base. Select TWO factors that most directly affect the quality of embedding-based retrieval. (Select TWO)

options.text: The embedding model used to encode queries and documents
The number of GPU cores on the vector search server
The chunking strategy and chunk size used during document ingestion
The temperature parameter of the generator LLM

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the embedding model determines the quality of vector representations — a domain-relevant model produces better representations than a generic one, directly impacting retrieval relevance.

**C** is correct because chunk size and strategy directly affect what information is in each retrievable unit — too small loses context, too large reduces precision.

**B** is incorrect because GPU cores affect search latency and throughput but not the semantic quality of retrieval results.

**D** is incorrect because the generator's temperature controls output randomness and has no effect on the retrieval step.

[Azure AI Search vector search overview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-overview)

questions.stem: A multi-turn customer support bot stores the full conversation history in the prompt. After 20 turns, users report that the bot starts ignoring earlier parts of the conversation. What is the MOST likely cause and the best remediation?

options.text: The LLM temperature is too high; reduce it to 0.1 to improve coherence
The conversation history exceeds the model's context window; implement conversation summarization to compress earlier turns
The system prompt is too long; shorten it to leave room for conversation history
The embedding model cannot represent long conversations; switch to a larger embedding model

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because LLMs have a fixed context window, and as conversation history grows, earlier turns get truncated or the model's attention is diluted across too many tokens — causing it to effectively ignore early context. The standard mitigation is to summarize older turns into a running summary.

**A** is incorrect because temperature affects the creativity and randomness of responses, not the model's ability to recall prior context.

**C** is incorrect because while shortening the system prompt helps marginally, it does not address the root cause of unbounded history growth.

**D** is incorrect because embeddings are not involved in multi-turn conversation history management for a chat completion model.

[Conversation history management in Azure OpenAI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/chatgpt)

questions.stem: An engineer is choosing between RAG and fine-tuning for a use case where an LLM needs to respond in a very specific brand voice and follow strict output format rules (e.g., always respond in JSON with specific field names). The underlying facts change infrequently. Which approach is MOST appropriate?

options.text: RAG only, because it is always better than fine-tuning for production systems
RAG only, because retrieved context can provide the format examples the model needs
Fine-tuning only, because the model needs to learn style and format patterns rather than new factual knowledge
Neither; use a rules engine to enforce output format instead of an LLM

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because fine-tuning is best suited for teaching a model behavioral patterns — style, tone, and structured output formats — that are consistent across many interactions. RAG addresses the problem of providing up-to-date factual knowledge at inference time, but it does not teach the model to reliably follow a new output schema.

**A** is incorrect because RAG is not universally superior to fine-tuning; the choice depends on whether the challenge is knowledge retrieval or behavioral adaptation.

**B** is incorrect because while few-shot examples in retrieved context can help, they are inconsistent and not as reliable as fine-tuning for strict format adherence at scale.

**D** is incorrect because a rules engine cannot handle the natural language understanding and generation component of the task.

[Fine-tuning vs RAG in Azure OpenAI Service](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/concepts/fine-tuning-considerations)

questions.stem: An Azure AI Search vector index is configured with approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search. A data scientist notices that some highly relevant documents are not being returned even though they exist in the index. What is the MOST likely explanation?

options.text: The documents were chunked with overlapping tokens, causing them to be deduplicated during indexing
ANN search trades recall for speed and may not return all true nearest neighbors; increasing the efSearch parameter or switching to exhaustive KNN may help
The embedding model used at query time differs from the one used at indexing time
Cosine similarity does not support documents longer than 512 tokens

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because approximate nearest neighbor algorithms (like HNSW) use graph-based shortcuts to achieve fast search at the cost of recall — they may miss some true nearest neighbors. Increasing the efSearch parameter explores more candidates and improves recall at the cost of latency.

**C** is also a real problem (mismatched embedding models would cause poor results), but a model mismatch would affect all queries uniformly, not just some documents selectively.

**A** is incorrect because chunk overlap does not cause deduplication during Azure AI Search indexing.

**D** is incorrect because cosine similarity has no document length limit; the 512-token limit applies to some embedding model inputs, not to the similarity metric itself.

[HNSW algorithm parameters in Azure AI Search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-how-to-create-index)

questions.stem: A data engineer is building a RAG pipeline and needs to extract text from a large collection of PDF files stored in Azure Blob Storage. Which Azure AI approach is MOST appropriate for processing these documents at scale?

options.text: Use a single-node Python script with PyPDF2 to extract text sequentially on a local machine
Store the PDFs directly in Azure AI Search using the Documents Index API without any extraction step
Use Azure AI Document Intelligence integrated with an Azure AI Search indexer skill set to extract and chunk text in parallel
Use Azure Machine Learning model serving to parse PDFs asynchronously via a REST endpoint

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because Azure AI Document Intelligence (formerly Form Recognizer) is purpose-built for extracting structured text from PDFs, and when combined with an Azure AI Search indexer skill set, it processes documents in parallel from Blob Storage at scale.

**A** is incorrect because a sequential single-node script will not scale to large document collections in a production pipeline.

**B** is incorrect because Azure AI Search cannot store raw binary PDFs as indexed documents; text extraction must happen before or during indexing.

**D** is incorrect because Azure ML model serving is designed for real-time inference, not bulk document processing pipelines.

[Azure AI Document Intelligence in Azure AI Search skill sets](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/cognitive-search-skill-document-intelligence-layout)

questions.stem: An engineer is creating an Azure AI Search index to serve as the retrieval component of a RAG pipeline. The index will store document text, embeddings, and metadata. Which field configuration is MANDATORY for the index to function?

options.text: A field named 'embedding' of type Collection(Edm.Single) configured as searchable
A field of type Edm.String marked as the document key
The index must use the PARQUET file format for the underlying storage
Change tracking must be disabled on the data source to prevent sync conflicts

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because every Azure AI Search index requires a key field of type Edm.String (or Edm.Int32) to uniquely identify each document. This key is used for all document operations including upserts and deletes.

**A** is incorrect because the vector field can be named anything — it does not have to be called 'embedding'. What matters is that the field is declared as a Collection(Edm.Single) with the correct dimensionality and vector search profile.

**C** is incorrect because Azure AI Search manages its own internal storage format; users do not configure PARQUET or Delta options.

**D** is incorrect because Azure AI Search indexers actually benefit from change-tracking policies on the data source to efficiently detect and index only changed documents.

[Create a vector index in Azure AI Search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-how-to-create-index)

questions.stem: A team is using LangChain's RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter to chunk a collection of technical manuals. They set chunk_size=512 and chunk_overlap=50. What does the chunk_overlap parameter accomplish?

options.text: It duplicates chunks that appear more than once in the document to improve recall
It ensures that each chunk shares 50 characters with the adjacent chunk, reducing the risk of splitting a sentence or concept across two chunks with no bridging context
It limits the maximum number of overlapping documents returned by the vector search
It sets the number of tokens by which embeddings from adjacent chunks are averaged together

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because chunk_overlap causes adjacent chunks to share a specified number of characters (or tokens) at their boundaries. This prevents important information from being lost when a concept or sentence spans a chunk boundary — the repeated text at the overlap ensures both chunks contain the bridging context.

**A** is incorrect because chunk overlap is about boundary bridging, not deduplication or content repetition across the corpus.

**C** is incorrect because retrieval top-k is a separate search parameter, unrelated to the text splitting configuration.

**D** is incorrect because embeddings are generated per chunk independently and are not averaged with neighboring chunks.

[LangChain text splitting and chunking strategies](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)

questions.stem: A team is preparing a dataset of customer support tickets for a RAG index. Select TWO data quality issues that are MOST important to address before embedding and indexing. (Select TWO)

options.text: Duplicate tickets that would create redundant chunks and skew retrieval results toward repeated content
Tickets written in multiple human languages, which should be normalized to English
Tickets that contain customer PII (names, account numbers) that could be exposed in LLM responses
Tickets with timestamps older than 5 years that may contain outdated information

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because duplicate content in the index wastes index capacity and causes retrieved results to over-represent repeated information, hurting the diversity and relevance of the context provided to the LLM.

**C** is correct because indexing PII means it can be surfaced in LLM-generated responses to other users, creating a serious privacy and compliance risk (GDPR, CCPA).

**B** is situationally relevant but not universally the most important — multilingual RAG is a valid design, and normalizing to English could lose nuance for non-English queries.

**D** is situationally relevant but depends on the use case — historical context can be valuable, and filtering by date can be handled as a metadata filter at retrieval time.

[Data preparation for RAG in Azure AI Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)

questions.stem: An engineer wants to generate embeddings for a batch of document chunks using the Azure OpenAI embeddings API and store the results in Azure AI Search. Which approach correctly generates embeddings in batch?

options.text: Call the Azure OpenAI Embeddings endpoint with a list of strings in the 'input' field: POST /openai/deployments/{deployment}/embeddings with {"input": [text1, text2, ...]}
Use the Azure OpenAI Chat Completions API with a system prompt asking the model to return embeddings
Use mlflow.log_param('embedding_model', 'text-embedding-ada-002') to record the model choice, then call the model directly via a separate REST client
Deploy a custom embedding model to Azure Machine Learning and call it with raw HTTP from a batch pipeline

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the Azure OpenAI Embeddings API accepts a list of strings in the 'input' field of the JSON request body — each string is embedded in a single API call, and the response returns a list of corresponding embedding vectors, enabling efficient batch processing.

**B** is incorrect because the Chat Completions API generates text responses, not embedding vectors. Embeddings require the dedicated Embeddings endpoint.

**C** is incorrect because mlflow.log_param only records metadata for experiment tracking; it does not generate embeddings.

**D** is not wrong in principle, but deploying a custom model to Azure ML adds unnecessary complexity when the Azure OpenAI Embeddings API already handles batch requests natively.

[Azure OpenAI embeddings API](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/embeddings)

questions.stem: A team is building a generative AI application that must answer questions about a company's internal HR policies. The policies change quarterly. Which architecture component ensures the LLM has access to the most current policy text without requiring model retraining?

options.text: A fine-tuned LLM trained on the latest policy documents each quarter
A larger LLM with more parameters to improve knowledge retention
An Azure AI Search vector index populated from current policy documents, used as the retrieval component in a RAG pipeline
An in-context learning approach where policies are pasted directly into the model weights

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because an Azure AI Search vector index populated from current documents can be refreshed whenever policies are updated, allowing a RAG pipeline to always serve the latest information without modifying the LLM.

**A** is incorrect because fine-tuning is expensive and slow — quarterly retraining would be cost-prohibitive and still introduces a lag between policy changes and model updates.

**B** is incorrect because larger models have more general knowledge but still have a training cutoff date and no awareness of internal company documents.

**D** is incorrect because information cannot be 'pasted into model weights' — in-context learning means providing examples in the prompt, which is limited by context window size and not persistent.

[RAG with Azure AI Search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/retrieval-augmented-generation-overview)

questions.stem: An engineer is designing an embedding strategy for a RAG system that will search across both scientific papers and short news headlines. The engineer considers using a single embedding model for both. What is the PRIMARY risk of this approach?

options.text: The embedding model may have a maximum token limit that excludes full scientific papers
A single generic embedding model may not capture domain-specific terminology from scientific literature, leading to poor retrieval quality for technical queries
Using one embedding model means the system cannot support cosine similarity search
A single model increases the index size unnecessarily

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a generic embedding model (e.g., trained on web text) may not adequately represent domain-specific vocabulary and concepts from scientific literature, resulting in poor semantic matching for technical queries. Domain-specific models produce better representations for specialized domains.

**A** is a real technical constraint (max token limits exist) but it is addressed by chunking, not by using multiple embedding models.

**C** is incorrect because the similarity metric is independent of how many embedding models are used.

**D** is incorrect because index size depends on the number of vectors and their dimensionality, not on the number of embedding models.

[Choose an embedding model in Azure AI Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/how-to/deploy-models-cohere-embed)

questions.stem: An Azure AI developer creates a push-model (direct index) vector index in Azure AI Search. How does this index type differ from a pull-model (indexer-driven) index in terms of data management?

options.text: A push-model index automatically syncs with a source data store; a pull-model index requires manual API calls to update
A push-model index requires the application to explicitly upsert and delete documents via the REST API; a pull-model index uses an indexer that automatically tracks changes in the data source
A push-model index only supports exact search; a pull-model index supports approximate nearest neighbor search
A push-model index stores raw text; a pull-model index stores pre-computed embeddings

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct and accurately describes the two indexing models. A push-model index is managed by the application — you call the Documents Index API to upsert or delete individual documents, giving you fine-grained control. A pull-model index is driven by an indexer that periodically polls or is triggered to pull changes from the data source automatically.

**A** is the reverse of the correct description — it incorrectly attributes automatic sync to the push model.

**C** is incorrect because the search algorithm (ANN vs exact KNN) is configurable independently of whether you use push or pull indexing.

**D** is incorrect because both indexing models can store embeddings; the distinction is purely about who manages document updates.

[Azure AI Search indexing models: push vs pull](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/search-what-is-data-import)

questions.stem: A team is deciding on a chunk size for their RAG system that indexes internal engineering runbooks. Select TWO consequences of using very small chunks (e.g., 50 tokens) compared to larger chunks (e.g., 512 tokens). (Select TWO)

options.text: Very small chunks lack sufficient context to be independently meaningful, causing the LLM to receive fragments that require surrounding text to be understood
Very small chunks result in higher retrieval precision because each chunk covers a single fact
Very small chunks increase the total number of vectors in the index, increasing storage and search cost
Very small chunks eliminate the need for chunk overlap

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a 50-token chunk is often too short to convey a complete thought — it may contain half a sentence or an incomplete instruction, making the retrieved context confusing to the LLM even if it is topically relevant.

**C** is correct because smaller chunks produce more chunks per document, multiplying the number of vectors that must be stored and searched, which increases both storage cost and query latency.

**B** is incorrect because very small chunks do not reliably increase precision — they may match a query on a superficial keyword but fail to provide useful information.

**D** is incorrect because overlap becomes even more important with very small chunks, since boundary-crossing information is more likely to be split across adjacent tiny chunks.

[Chunking strategies for vector search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-integrated-vectorization-ai-studio)

questions.stem: A data scientist is comparing a small language model (e.g., Phi-3 Mini) and a large language model (e.g., GPT-4) for a RAG chatbot. The smaller model is significantly cheaper. For which type of query would the smaller model be MOST likely to underperform?

options.text: Simple factual lookups where the answer is directly present in the retrieved context
Queries requiring multi-step reasoning, synthesis across multiple retrieved passages, or complex instruction following
Queries that require generating short, single-sentence responses
Queries submitted in English about well-known general topics

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because larger models have significantly greater capacity for complex reasoning, instruction following, and synthesis tasks. For simple fact retrieval where the answer appears verbatim in the context, a small model often performs comparably. Multi-step reasoning, synthesis across documents, and nuanced instruction following are where scale differences are most pronounced.

**A** is incorrect because simple factual lookups are well within the capability of small models when the answer is present in the retrieved context.

**C** is incorrect because generating short responses is one of the easiest tasks for any LLM, regardless of size.

**D** is incorrect because general English queries about common topics are also well within the capability of small models.

[Azure AI Foundry model catalog](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/how-to/model-catalog-overview)

questions.stem: In a RAG pipeline, what is the role of the embedding model at query time?

options.text: It generates the final answer to the user's question
It reranks the retrieved documents by relevance before they are passed to the LLM
It converts the user's query into a vector so it can be compared against the indexed document vectors via similarity search
It tokenizes the retrieved documents into sub-word units for the LLM

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because at query time, the same embedding model used during document ingestion is applied to the user's query to produce a query vector. This vector is then compared against the stored document chunk vectors using a similarity metric to find the most semantically relevant chunks.

**A** is incorrect because generating the final answer is the role of the generator LLM, not the embedding model.

**B** is incorrect because reranking is a separate, optional step that happens after initial retrieval, typically using a cross-encoder model.

**D** is incorrect because tokenization for the LLM is handled by the LLM's own tokenizer, not the embedding model.

[RAG with Azure AI Search and Azure OpenAI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/retrieval-augmented-generation-overview)

questions.stem: An engineer is configuring an Azure AI Search vector index and must choose between cosine similarity and dot product for their index. The embedding model they are using does NOT normalize output vectors to unit length. Which similarity metric is safer to use and why?

options.text: Dot product, because it is always faster to compute than cosine similarity
Cosine similarity, because it normalizes for vector magnitude, making the similarity score reflect only the angular (directional) similarity regardless of vector scale
L2 distance, because it accounts for both direction and magnitude and is always the most accurate
Dot product, because it gives higher scores to longer documents which typically contain more information

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because when embedding vectors are not unit-normalized, dot product scores are influenced by both direction and magnitude. Two vectors pointing in the same direction (same semantic meaning) will have different dot product scores if one has a larger magnitude — introducing a length or frequency bias. Cosine similarity divides by the product of the magnitudes, normalizing the score to reflect only the angular difference.

**A** is incorrect because the speed difference is negligible and is not a valid reason to compromise retrieval accuracy.

**C** is incorrect because L2 distance also captures magnitude differences and is generally less appropriate than cosine similarity for semantic text matching.

**D** is incorrect because preferring longer documents based on magnitude is a bias, not a feature — longer documents are not necessarily more relevant to a given query.

[Similarity metrics in Azure AI Search vector search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-ranking)

questions.stem: A team is building a generative AI application where users upload personal financial documents and ask questions. They need to ensure that one user's documents are never retrievable in another user's session. Which design approach correctly implements this isolation in Azure AI Search?

options.text: Create a single shared index and rely on the LLM's system prompt to instruct it not to share other users' data
Store a user_id metadata field in each document and apply a user_id filter on every search call, enforced server-side by the application layer
Assign each user a different embedding model so their vectors are in incomparable spaces
Rely on Microsoft Entra ID to automatically filter search results to only the calling user's documents without explicit index-level configuration

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the standard pattern for multi-tenant RAG with Azure AI Search is to store user_id (or tenant_id) as a filterable metadata field and pass it as a mandatory filter parameter on every search call. The application layer must enforce that a user's session can only supply their own user_id as a filter value.

**A** is incorrect because relying on the LLM's instruction following for data isolation is insecure — the LLM can be prompted to ignore such instructions via adversarial inputs.

**C** is incorrect because assigning different embedding models per user is operationally impractical at scale and does not reliably enforce data isolation at the retrieval layer.

**D** is incorrect because while Azure AI Search supports security trimming via Microsoft Entra ID, it must be explicitly configured with user-specific permissions; it does not happen automatically without setup.

[Security trimming in Azure AI Search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/search-security-trimming-for-azure-search)

questions.stem: A product team wants to implement a generative AI feature where the chatbot proactively summarizes the conversation every 5 turns to stay within the context window while preserving key information. What is the main trade-off of this approach?

options.text: Summarization adds latency from the additional LLM call, and the summary may lose specific details that are needed to answer follow-up questions
Summarization permanently fine-tunes the model on the conversation, causing it to forget its original training
Summarization prevents the use of retrieval-augmented generation in subsequent turns
Summarization increases the context window requirement, making the approach counterproductive

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because rolling summarization introduces two costs: (1) latency from the additional LLM call needed to generate the summary, and (2) information loss — a summary compresses detail, and specific facts from earlier in the conversation may be omitted yet become relevant in later turns.

**B** is incorrect because LLM inference does not modify model weights — summarization is just another inference call.

**C** is incorrect because RAG retrieval operates independently of how conversation history is managed.

**D** is incorrect because summarization reduces, not increases, the amount of context being maintained — that is precisely the goal.

[Conversation history management in Azure OpenAI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/chatgpt)

questions.stem: An engineer needs to split a large collection of Markdown documentation files into chunks using LangChain. The documentation uses headers (# H1, ## H2) to organize sections. Which text splitter is MOST appropriate for preserving section boundaries?

options.text: CharacterTextSplitter with separator='\n'
TokenTextSplitter that splits on the 512th token boundary
RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter with chunk_size=100
MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter that splits based on Markdown header hierarchy

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter understands the Markdown heading hierarchy and creates chunks that align with document sections, preserving the logical structure of the document so each chunk is a coherent section rather than an arbitrary character-count slice.

**A** is incorrect because splitting on newlines without understanding section boundaries creates inconsistently sized and semantically arbitrary chunks.

**B** is incorrect because splitting at fixed token boundaries has no awareness of Markdown structure or content organization.

**C** is incorrect because RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter splits on character count recursively without respecting Markdown header structure.

[Document loaders and text splitters](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)

questions.stem: An engineer is building an embedding pipeline for a large corpus using the Azure OpenAI embeddings API (text-embedding-3-small deployment). To maximize throughput, they want to embed multiple texts in a single API call. Which parameter controls the number of texts per request?

options.text: Pass a list of strings to the 'input' field of the request payload
Set the batch_size parameter on the deployment configuration
Use the num_threads parameter in the client call
Configure the max_tokens parameter to accommodate multiple documents

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the Azure OpenAI embeddings API accepts a list of strings in the 'input' field of the JSON request body — each string in the list is embedded in a single API call, and the response returns a list of corresponding embedding vectors.

**B** is incorrect because batch_size is not a per-request parameter you configure on the deployment itself.

**C** is incorrect because num_threads controls client-side concurrency for making multiple HTTP requests, not the number of texts sent in a single request.

**D** is incorrect because max_tokens controls the maximum length of each text input, not how many texts are sent per request.

[Azure OpenAI embeddings API reference](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/embeddings)

questions.stem: A team discovers that their document corpus contains many near-duplicate product descriptions (slightly reworded versions of the same content). What is the BEST approach to handle this before indexing?

options.text: Index all near-duplicates to maximize recall; the vector search will score them similarly and the top result will still be correct
Apply MinHash LSH or embedding-based cosine similarity deduplication to identify and remove near-duplicate chunks before indexing
Increase the similarity threshold in vector search to filter out redundant results at query time
Use a larger chunk size to merge near-duplicate content into single chunks automatically

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because near-duplicates inflate the index unnecessarily, and at query time they consume multiple slots in the top-k results with essentially identical content, crowding out diverse relevant chunks. MinHash LSH is efficient for text deduplication at scale; embedding-based cosine similarity can catch semantic near-duplicates.

**A** is incorrect because while the top result may be correct, redundant chunks waste retrieval slots and reduce the diversity of context provided to the LLM.

**C** is incorrect because increasing the similarity threshold changes query-time filtering but does not eliminate redundancy from the index itself.

**D** is incorrect because larger chunk sizes do not merge semantically similar but textually non-adjacent documents.

[Data preparation for RAG in Azure AI Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)

questions.stem: A team is building a RAG pipeline for a customer support system. Customer emails are the source documents. Select TWO preprocessing steps that are MOST important before embedding. (Select TWO)

options.text: Remove email headers (From:, To:, Subject:, Date:) and quoted reply chains to avoid embedding boilerplate and repeated content
Convert all text to uppercase for consistent tokenization
Replace customer names, email addresses, and account numbers with placeholder tokens to prevent PII from being indexed
Translate all non-English emails to English before embedding

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because email headers and quoted reply chains are noise — they do not contain useful semantic content and will degrade embedding quality and waste index space. Removing them ensures the embedding represents only substantive content.

**C** is correct because indexing PII exposes it to being surfaced in future LLM responses to other users, creating a serious privacy and compliance risk under GDPR and CCPA.

**B** is incorrect because converting to uppercase can hurt tokenization for modern subword tokenizers (like BPE or SentencePiece) and provides no quality benefit.

**D** is situationally useful but not universally necessary — multilingual embedding models can handle multiple languages, and translation introduces potential errors and loses nuance.

[Data preparation for RAG in Azure AI Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)

questions.stem: A data engineer configures an Azure AI Search indexer connected to an Azure Blob Storage data source. Documents added since the last indexer run are not being picked up on subsequent runs. What is the MOST likely reason?

options.text: The indexer is pointing to a different storage container than where new files are uploaded
No change-detection policy is configured on the data source; without it the indexer cannot identify which blobs are new or modified
The Azure Blob Storage container contains more than 1 million files, exceeding the indexer limit
The indexer uses soft-delete detection, which is incompatible with incremental indexing

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Azure AI Search indexers require a change-detection policy on the data source to identify new or modified documents since the last run. For Azure Blob Storage, this is typically the High Watermark policy based on the blob's lastModified timestamp. Without it, the indexer may not detect new additions.

**A** is a plausible configuration error, but it would result in no documents being indexed at all, not just new ones being missed.

**C** is incorrect because there is no documented hard limit of 1 million files that causes this specific incremental-detection behavior.

**D** is incorrect because soft-delete detection and change-detection are complementary features; soft-delete detection does not conflict with incremental indexing.

[Change detection policies for Azure AI Search indexers](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/search-howto-index-changed-deleted-blobs)

questions.stem: An Azure AI Foundry agent must always answer in JSON with fields `answer` and `citations`. Where should that requirement be placed for the MOST consistent behavior across all turns?

options.text: In the system prompt or equivalent top-level instructions
Only in the final user message of each turn
Inside retrieved documents injected as context
Inside a tool description field for one of the available tools

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because persistent formatting and behavioral constraints belong in the system-level instructions, which are applied to every turn. This ensures consistent output format regardless of what the user asks.

**B** is incorrect because placing requirements only in the final user message means they must be repeated on every turn — they are not persistent across the conversation.

**C** is incorrect because retrieved documents are dynamic context; format requirements injected via retrieval would appear inconsistently and could be overridden by other context.

**D** is incorrect because tool descriptions explain what a tool does, not how the overall agent should format its responses.

[System messages in Azure OpenAI Service](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/concepts/system-message)

questions.stem: A prompt engineer keeps adding long persona text that does not affect task quality but consumes tokens. What is the BEST improvement?

options.text: Add more adjectives so the agent stays in character
Move the persona text into every retrieved chunk
Keep only instructions that materially change agent behavior and remove decorative prompt text
Replace task instructions with chain-of-thought requirements

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because effective prompt engineering emphasizes useful signal in the context window. Extra decorative persona text consumes tokens without helping task completion and may dilute the model's attention from the actual instructions.

**A** is incorrect because adding more adjectives worsens the problem by increasing token consumption without improving task quality.

**B** is incorrect because inserting persona text into retrieved documents is both ineffective and wasteful — retrieved context should contain domain knowledge, not persona instructions.

**D** is incorrect because replacing task instructions with chain-of-thought prompts changes the nature of the prompt rather than addressing the token waste problem.

[System message design best practices](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/concepts/system-message)

questions.stem: A team wants a managed semantic search index that stays synchronized with a source data store automatically. Which Azure AI Search feature BEST fits?

options.text: A pull-model indexer with a scheduled trigger and change-detection policy
A Jupyter notebook that re-indexes data on demand
An Azure SQL result cache
An Azure Machine Learning model registry alias

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Azure AI Search pull-model indexers with scheduled triggers and change-detection policies automatically keep the index synchronized with the source data store, requiring no manual intervention.

**B** is incorrect because a Jupyter notebook requires manual execution and is not a managed, automated synchronization solution.

**C** is incorrect because an Azure SQL result cache stores query results for performance, not a semantic search index over AI-generated content.

**D** is incorrect because an Azure Machine Learning model registry alias refers to a specific model version, not a data synchronization or search mechanism.

[Azure AI Search indexer scheduling](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/search-howto-schedule-indexers)

questions.stem: Search quality drops because each chunk contains many unrelated topics from long documents. Which change is MOST likely to help?

options.text: Use a chunking strategy that preserves local semantic coherence, such as semantic or header-based splitting
Increase the Azure AI Search service tier to add more search replicas
Disable vector search and use only keyword full-text search
Store every document as one very large chunk to preserve all context

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because better chunking improves retrieval quality — when each chunk represents one coherent topic, its embedding captures a clear semantic signal rather than a blend of unrelated topics, making it easier to match to relevant queries.

**B** is incorrect because adding search replicas improves query throughput and availability, not the semantic quality of retrieval results.

**C** is incorrect because disabling vector search and using only keyword search would lose semantic matching capability and would not solve the incoherent-chunk problem.

**D** is incorrect because very large chunks that span many topics produce poor embeddings and hurt both precision and recall.

[Chunking strategies in Azure AI Search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-integrated-vectorization-ai-studio)

questions.stem: A retrieval system needs to balance semantic similarity with exact keyword matching for product codes. Select TWO approaches that BEST help. (Select TWO)

options.text: Use hybrid retrieval that combines dense vector search with sparse BM25 keyword search
Use a cross-encoder re-ranker to improve the final ordering of retrieved results
Remove all metadata fields from the index
Increase the model temperature during generation

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because hybrid retrieval combines dense vector search (semantic similarity) with sparse BM25 keyword search, ensuring exact token matches like product codes are found while also capturing semantic relevance.

**B** is correct because a cross-encoder re-ranker evaluates query-document pairs jointly and can significantly improve the final relevance ordering of retrieved results before they are passed to the LLM.

**C** is incorrect because removing metadata fields reduces the filtering and ranking signals available during search.

**D** is incorrect because generation temperature controls output diversity, not retrieval ranking quality.

[Hybrid search in Azure AI Search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/hybrid-search-overview)

questions.stem: A support agent must remember important user preferences across separate sessions days apart. Which memory design is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Persist long-term memory outside the immediate chat window in an external store and retrieve it when relevant at the start of each session
Keep all historical turns in the active prompt indefinitely
Rely only on the model's parametric knowledge from pretraining
Use a larger Azure OpenAI model with a bigger context window

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because cross-session memory requires external persistence (e.g., Azure Cosmos DB or Azure AI Search) and selective retrieval at the start of each new session. This is the only approach that survives the end of a chat context.

**B** is incorrect because keeping all historical turns in-context is expensive and eventually impossible once accumulated history exceeds the context window limit.

**C** is incorrect because the model's pretraining knowledge does not include user-specific preferences or conversation history.

**D** is incorrect because even a very large context window does not provide cross-session persistence — the context is reset at the start of each new session.

[Agent memory in Azure AI Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/agents)

questions.stem: Conversation history is causing prompt bloat, but the agent still needs prior decisions. Which technique BEST addresses this?

options.text: Summarize older turns into a compact state representation and replace them in the prompt
Duplicate the entire chat transcript in the system prompt
Delete all history after every message
Move the history into a separate tool call result

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because summarization preserves key facts and decisions while significantly reducing token usage — older detailed turns are replaced with a compact summary, keeping the prompt within context window limits.

**B** is incorrect because duplicating the transcript in the system prompt worsens the prompt bloat problem rather than solving it.

**C** is incorrect because deleting all history after every message means the agent loses useful context about prior decisions that may be needed for coherent multi-turn behavior.

**D** is incorrect because moving history into a tool call result does not reduce the total number of tokens consumed — the content must still be included somewhere in the context.

[Context window management for Azure OpenAI agents](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/concepts/context-window)

questions.stem: What is the PRIMARY value of exposing a well-defined tool schema to an Azure AI Foundry agent?

options.text: It lets the model understand the tool's inputs and outputs so it can call the tool more reliably and with correctly formatted arguments
It guarantees the model will never hallucinate
It replaces authentication and governance controls for the tool
It eliminates the need for system prompt instructions

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a clear tool schema (name, description, input parameters, types) improves reliable tool invocation by making the expected interface explicit to the model, reducing malformed calls and improving the agent's ability to select the right tool for a given task.

**B** is incorrect because tool schemas help with structured calling but do not prevent hallucination in the model's reasoning or generated content.

**C** is incorrect because tool schemas describe the interface; authentication, authorization, and governance controls are separate concerns that must be enforced independently.

**D** is incorrect because tool schemas and system prompt instructions serve different purposes and are both needed for reliable agent behavior.

[Function calling in Azure OpenAI Service](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/function-calling)

questions.stem: A governed AI agent should query sensitive data sources only through approved interfaces. Which design BEST supports this goal?

options.text: Expose curated Azure AI Foundry tools or Azure Functions with Microsoft Entra ID authorization instead of giving the model unrestricted direct data access
Let the model generate arbitrary SQL against every database in the environment
Embed data source credentials directly in the system prompt
Bypass Microsoft Entra ID controls and read from cloud storage directly via anonymous access

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because exposing only curated, governed tools (e.g., Azure Functions with Entra ID authorization) constrains what the agent can query, enables policy enforcement, and provides an auditable access layer between the model and sensitive data.

**B** is incorrect because allowing the model to generate arbitrary SQL against any database creates an uncontrolled attack surface and violates the principle of least privilege.

**C** is incorrect because embedding credentials in system prompts risks credential exposure through prompt injection attacks or accidental logging.

**D** is incorrect because bypassing authentication controls removes all security enforcement and violates governance requirements.

[Responsible AI for Azure AI Foundry agents](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/responsible-ai)

questions.stem: A team is integrating external tools into an Azure AI Foundry agent. Select TWO good practices. (Select TWO)

options.text: Validate tool inputs against the declared schema before executing the tool
Return clear, structured error information so the agent can recover or ask the user for clarification
Hide tool side effects from logs to reduce storage costs
Make every tool accept one unstructured free-text string as its only parameter

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because input validation against the declared schema prevents malformed arguments from reaching the underlying system and allows the agent to detect and recover from errors early.

**B** is correct because returning structured error messages (rather than opaque failures) allows the agent to understand what went wrong and either retry with corrected arguments or inform the user.

**C** is incorrect because hiding tool side effects from logs undermines observability and auditability, which are critical for debugging and compliance.

**D** is incorrect because accepting only a single free-text string removes the schema structure that helps the model call the tool correctly and makes input validation impossible.

[Function calling best practices in Azure OpenAI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/function-calling)

questions.stem: Which statement BEST describes token budgeting in a production Azure AI Foundry agent?

options.text: You must account for system instructions, retrieved context, tool call results, and conversation history all competing within the model's context window limit
Only user messages count toward context length
Tokens matter only for cost, not for model context limits
Azure AI Foundry caching eliminates context window limits

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because all prompt components — system instructions, retrieved context, tool call inputs and outputs, and conversation history — consume capacity from the same context window. Effective token budgeting requires managing all of these together.

**B** is incorrect because system messages, assistant turns, tool results, and retrieved context all count toward the context window, not just user messages.

**C** is incorrect because exceeding the context window causes errors or truncation regardless of cost considerations.

**D** is incorrect because Azure AI Foundry's prompt caching (where available) reduces cost and latency but does not increase or remove the model's context window limit.

[Azure OpenAI token limits and context windows](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/concepts/models)

questions.stem: An Azure AI Foundry agent frequently overflows its context window after retrieving too many chunks. Which mitigation is MOST targeted?

options.text: Increase the response temperature to generate shorter answers
Reduce the number of retrieved chunks (lower top-k), apply a relevance score threshold, and summarize or prune lower-value conversation history
Store chunks in a secondary storage account instead of Azure AI Search
Disable citations in the system prompt

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because context overflow from retrieval is best addressed by reducing the volume of retrieved content (lower top-k, add a minimum relevance score threshold) and by compacting conversation history through summarization of older turns. These directly target the sources of token consumption.

**A** is incorrect because temperature controls output creativity and randomness, not the number of input tokens consumed by retrieved chunks.

**C** is incorrect because where chunks are stored has no effect on how many tokens are consumed when they are retrieved and injected into the context.

**D** is incorrect because disabling citations reduces a small portion of output tokens but does not address the primary issue of too much retrieved content in the input context.

[RAG context management in Azure AI Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)
